title: Section 80C Tax Saving Options 2026 — Complete Guide with ₹ Examples and Comparison
meta_description: Section 80C tax saving guide 2026: Compare PPF, ELSS, NPS, FD with ₹ examples. Salary-wise scenarios, post-tax returns, and allocation strategies for every Indian.
excerpt: You earn ₹12 lakh. You can legally pay ₹46,800 LESS in tax. This guide shows you exactly how — with real ₹ calculations for every salary level, post-tax return comparisons, and the optimal 80C allocation for your age and risk profile.
content:
Updated April 2026 | Rates as per Finance Act 2025 | AY 2026-27

You earn ₹12 lakh. You can legally pay ₹46,800 LESS in tax. Here's how.

Every year, lakhs of salaried Indians scramble in March to "save tax." They buy random insurance policies, dump money into tax-saving FDs, and hope for the best. The result? They save tax but lose money on bad investments.

This guide fixes that. We'll show you every Section 80C option with real numbers, compare post-tax returns (something no other guide does), and give you a clear allocation strategy based on your salary, age, and goals.

No jargon. No product pushing. Just math.

Disclaimer: This guide is for educational purposes. Tax laws change — consult a qualified Chartered Accountant (CA) for personalised advice. For official rules, refer to incometax.gov.in.

What is Section 80C? The Basics

Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961 lets you reduce your taxable income by up to ₹1,50,000 per financial year by investing in specified instruments.

Here's what that means in plain Hindi-English:

  • You earn ₹10 lakh
  • You invest ₹1.5 lakh in 80C-eligible options
  • Government taxes you on ₹8.5 lakh instead of ₹10 lakh
  • You save ₹31,200 in tax (at the 20% slab + cess)

Three Things You Must Know

  1. ₹1.5 lakh is the combined limit — PPF + ELSS + EPF + insurance premiums + tuition fees all share this one bucket. You can't claim ₹1.5L in PPF AND ₹1.5L in ELSS.
  2. 80C only works under the OLD tax regime — If you choose the new regime (default from FY 2024-25), you get ZERO benefit from 80C. More on this below.
  3. Your EPF contribution already counts — If you're salaried, your Employee Provident Fund contribution (12% of basic) is automatically under 80C. So if your basic is ₹5L, ₹60,000 is already used up. You have ₹90,000 left to allocate.

Complete List of Section 80C Eligible Investments

InvestmentSectionMaximum Limit
Employee Provident Fund (EPF)80C₹1.5L (shared)
Public Provident Fund (PPF)80C₹1.5L/year deposit cap
Equity Linked Savings Scheme (ELSS)80CNo cap (but deduction capped at ₹1.5L)
National Pension System (NPS) — Tier 180CCD(1) within 80C + 80CCD(1B) extra ₹50K₹1.5L + ₹50K extra
National Savings Certificate (NSC)80CNo investment cap
Tax-Saving Fixed Deposit (5-year)80CNo investment cap
Senior Citizens Savings Scheme (SCSS)80C₹30L cap
Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana (SSY)80C₹1.5L/year deposit cap
Life Insurance Premium80CUp to 10% of sum assured
Tuition Fees (max 2 children)80CActual fees paid
Home Loan Principal Repayment80CActual principal component
Stamp Duty & Registration80CYear of purchase only
NABARD Rural Bonds80C₹1.5L (shared)
Infrastructure Bonds (if notified)80C₹1.5L (shared)

Now let's focus on the 10 options that actually matter for most people.

The Big 10: Section 80C Options Compared

This is the comparison table you'll keep coming back to. Bookmark it.

OptionReturns (Current)Lock-in PeriodRisk LevelTax on ReturnsBest For
PPF7.1% p.a.15 yearsZero (Govt.)Tax-FREE (EEE)Conservative, long-term
ELSS Mutual Funds10-15% p.a. (historical)3 yearsHigh (equity)12.5% LTCG above ₹1.25LAggressive, wealth creation
NPS (Tier 1)9-14% p.a. (equity allocation)Till age 60Medium-High60% tax-exempt at exitRetirement + extra ₹50K deduction
NSC7.7% p.a.5 yearsZero (Govt.)Fully taxableMedium-term, safe
Tax-Saving FD6.5-7.5% p.a.5 yearsZero (Bank)Fully taxableLazy option, familiar
SCSS8.2% p.a.5 yearsZero (Govt.)Fully taxableSenior citizens (60+)
Sukanya Samriddhi8.2% p.a.21 years (or marriage after 18)Zero (Govt.)Tax-FREE (EEE)Parents of daughters (under 10)
Life Insurance4-6% (endowment) or N/A (term)Policy termZeroTax-free (Section 10(10D))Protection (term plan only)
Tuition FeesN/A (expense, not investment)N/AN/AN/AParents with school/college kids
Home Loan PrincipalN/A (debt repayment)N/AN/AN/AHome buyers (existing EMI)

PPF — The Gold Standard of Safe Tax Saving

Public Provident Fund is India's favourite tax-saving option, and for good reason:

  • EEE status: Investment is deductible (80C) + interest is tax-free + maturity is tax-free. This is the holy trinity of tax benefits.
  • Current rate: 7.1% p.a. (compounded annually, revised quarterly by govt)
  • Lock-in: 15 years (partial withdrawal from year 7, loan from year 3)
  • Deposit limit: ₹500 minimum to ₹1,50,000 maximum per year

Pro tip: Deposit before the 5th of every month. PPF interest is calculated on the minimum balance between the 5th and end of month. Depositing on April 6th instead of April 4th costs you one full month of interest.

Calculate your PPF maturity amount →

ELSS — Highest Returns, Shortest Lock-in

Equity Linked Savings Schemes are mutual funds that invest in stocks. Among all 80C options, ELSS has:

  • Shortest lock-in: Just 3 years (vs 5 years for FD, 15 years for PPF)
  • Highest return potential: Top ELSS funds have delivered 12-18% CAGR over 10+ years
  • Market risk: Returns are not guaranteed. You could lose money in bad years.

Tax on ELSS returns: Long-term capital gains (LTCG) above ₹1.25 lakh per year are taxed at 12.5%. So if you invest ₹1.5L and it grows to ₹2.5L in 3 years, your ₹1L gain is below the ₹1.25L threshold — completely tax-free.

Best ELSS strategy: Don't invest ₹1.5L in January as a lump sum. Start a monthly SIP of ₹12,500. This averages out market ups and downs.

See how much your ELSS SIP can grow →

NPS — The Double Deduction Power Play

National Pension System is unique because it gives you two separate deductions:

  • ₹1.5L under Section 80CCD(1) — this is WITHIN the 80C limit (shared bucket)
  • ₹50,000 EXTRA under Section 80CCD(1B) — this is OVER AND ABOVE the ₹1.5L limit

So with NPS, your total deduction can be ₹2,00,000 instead of ₹1,50,000. At the 30% tax bracket, that extra ₹50K saves you ₹15,600 more in tax.

The catch: Your money is locked till age 60. At maturity, 60% of the corpus is tax-exempt (you can withdraw it). The remaining 40% must buy an annuity (pension), and the pension income is taxable.

NPS returns depend on your allocation:

  • Aggressive (75% equity): 10-14% historical returns
  • Moderate (50% equity): 9-11%
  • Conservative (25% equity): 8-9%

Project your NPS retirement corpus →

NSC — The Boring-But-Reliable Option

National Savings Certificate is a post office savings scheme:

  • Rate: 7.7% p.a. (compounded annually but paid at maturity)
  • Lock-in: 5 years (no premature withdrawal)
  • Tax quirk: The interest is taxable, BUT the accrued interest of years 1-4 can be claimed as a fresh 80C deduction (it gets reinvested). Only year 5 interest is "net new" taxable income.

Tax-Saving FD — The Path of Least Resistance

Every bank offers 5-year tax-saving fixed deposits:

  • Rate: 6.5-7.5% depending on bank (senior citizens get 0.5% extra)
  • Lock-in: 5 years (NO premature withdrawal, unlike regular FDs)
  • Tax: Interest is FULLY taxable at your slab rate. TDS is deducted annually.

Why we call it the lazy option: It's the easiest to open (5 minutes in your banking app) but gives the worst post-tax returns. A tax-saving FD at 7% gives you only 4.9% after tax if you're in the 30% bracket. Your money barely beats inflation.

Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana — Best Option (If You Have a Daughter)

SSY is India's best-kept tax-saving secret:

  • Rate: 8.2% p.a. — highest among all government small savings
  • Tax: EEE (same as PPF — completely tax-free)
  • Eligibility: Only for girl child under 10 years (max 2 accounts)
  • Lock-in: Till daughter turns 21 (50% withdrawal after 18 for education/marriage)

If you have a daughter under 10, this should be your FIRST 80C choice. 8.2% tax-free is unbeatable in the risk-free category.

Life Insurance — Buy Term, Ignore the Rest

This is where most Indians waste their 80C limit. Here's the truth:

  • Endowment/money-back plans give 4-6% returns. That's WORSE than a savings account after inflation.
  • ULIPs have high charges and complex structures. Most underperform direct mutual funds.
  • Term insurance is the only life insurance worth buying — ₹1 crore cover for ₹10,000-15,000/year premium. Pure protection, no investment component.

Rule: Buy a term plan for protection. Use the rest of your 80C limit for PPF/ELSS/NPS. Never buy insurance for "tax saving."

The Money Math — Salary-Wise Tax Scenarios

This is the section no other guide gives you. Real calculations for 5 salary levels, comparing Old vs New regime.

Assumptions: Salaried individual, metro city, standard deduction ₹75,000, no other income. FY 2025-26 (AY 2026-27) rates. Health & education cess at 4%.

Scenario 1: ₹7.5 Lakh Salary — New Regime Wins

ComponentOld RegimeNew Regime
Gross Salary₹7,50,000₹7,50,000
Standard Deduction₹50,000₹75,000
Section 80C (EPF + PPF)₹1,50,000Not applicable
Taxable Income₹5,50,000₹6,75,000
Tax Before Cess₹22,500₹22,500
Section 87A Rebate₹12,500 (if taxable ≤ ₹5L: NO)₹25,000 (if taxable ≤ ₹7L: YES)
Tax After Rebate₹22,500₹0
Cess (4%)₹900₹0
Total Tax₹23,400₹0

Verdict: At ₹7.5L, the new regime gives you ZERO tax thanks to the ₹75K standard deduction + ₹25K rebate under Section 87A. No 80C investment needed. Don't waste money on tax-saving FDs at this salary.

Scenario 2: ₹10 Lakh Salary — Old Regime Starts Winning

ComponentOld RegimeNew Regime
Gross Salary₹10,00,000₹10,00,000
Standard Deduction₹50,000₹75,000
Section 80C₹1,50,000Not applicable
Section 80D (Health Insurance)₹25,000Not applicable
Taxable Income₹7,75,000₹9,25,000
Tax Calculated₹55,000₹52,500
Cess (4%)₹2,200₹2,100
Total Tax₹57,200₹54,600

Verdict: At ₹10L with only 80C + 80D, the new regime still wins by ₹2,600. But if you add HRA exemption (₹1.2L for metro rent), old regime tax drops to about ₹42,100 — saving you ₹12,500. Old regime wins once you add HRA.

Scenario 3: ₹12 Lakh Salary — The Sweet Spot for Old Regime

ComponentOld RegimeNew Regime
Gross Salary₹12,00,000₹12,00,000
HRA Exemption₹1,44,000Not applicable
Standard Deduction₹50,000₹75,000
Section 80C₹1,50,000Not applicable
80CCD(1B) — NPS Extra₹50,000Not applicable
80D (Health Insurance)₹25,000Not applicable
Total Deductions₹4,19,000₹75,000
Taxable Income₹7,81,000₹11,25,000
Tax Calculated₹56,200₹93,750
Cess (4%)₹2,248₹3,750
Total Tax₹58,448₹97,500
You save ₹39,052 with Old Regime

Verdict: At ₹12L with HRA + 80C + NPS + 80D, the old regime saves you almost ₹39,000. This is the sweet spot where most middle-class salaried Indians should seriously consider the old regime.

Compare Old vs New Regime for YOUR exact salary →

Scenario 4: ₹15 Lakh Salary — Old Regime Wins Big (If You Have Deductions)

ComponentOld RegimeNew Regime
Gross Salary₹15,00,000₹15,00,000
HRA Exemption₹1,80,000Not applicable
Standard Deduction₹50,000₹75,000
Section 80C₹1,50,000Not applicable
80CCD(1B) — NPS Extra₹50,000Not applicable
80D₹25,000Not applicable
Home Loan Interest (80EEA/24b)₹2,00,000Not applicable
Total Deductions₹6,55,000₹75,000
Taxable Income₹8,45,000₹14,25,000
Tax Calculated₹73,000₹1,50,000
Cess (4%)₹2,920₹6,000
Total Tax₹75,920₹1,56,000
You save ₹80,080 with Old Regime

Verdict: At ₹15L with ₹6.55L in deductions (HRA + 80C + NPS + 80D + home loan), old regime saves over ₹80,000. But you need ALL those deductions. If you only have 80C and nothing else, new regime might still win.

Scenario 5: ₹20 Lakh+ Salary — Do the Math, Don't Assume

ComponentOld Regime (Max Deductions)New Regime
Gross Salary₹20,00,000₹20,00,000
HRA Exemption₹2,40,000Not applicable
Standard Deduction₹50,000₹75,000
Section 80C₹1,50,000Not applicable
80CCD(1B)₹50,000Not applicable
80D (Self + Parents 60+)₹75,000Not applicable
Home Loan Interest (Sec 24b)₹2,00,000Not applicable
Total Deductions₹7,65,000₹75,000
Taxable Income₹12,35,000₹19,25,000
Tax Calculated₹1,72,000₹2,97,500
Cess (4%)₹6,880₹11,900
Total Tax₹1,78,880₹3,09,400
You save ₹1,30,520 with Old Regime (with all deductions)

Verdict: At ₹20L+, old regime wins by over ₹1.3 lakh — but ONLY if you have ₹7.65L+ in legitimate deductions. If your deductions are less than ₹4L, run the numbers for your specific situation.

Use our income tax calculator to compare both regimes for your salary →

Post-Tax Returns: The Comparison Nobody Shows You

This is the most important table in this entire guide. Every website compares "returns" but nobody shows you what you ACTUALLY keep after tax.

Assumptions: 30% tax bracket (₹15L+ income), ₹1.5L invested, held for full lock-in period.

InvestmentPre-Tax ReturnTax Treatment on ReturnsEffective Post-Tax Return₹1.5L Grows To (10 Years)
PPF7.1%Completely tax-free (EEE)7.1%₹2,98,900
Sukanya Samriddhi8.2%Completely tax-free (EEE)8.2%₹3,30,800
ELSS (Top Fund)12%12.5% LTCG above ₹1.25L~10.6%₹4,10,100
NPS (Aggressive)10%60% exempt; 40% annuity taxed~8.5-9%₹3,52,300
NSC7.7%Interest fully taxable at slab5.4%₹2,36,500
Tax-Saving FD7%Interest fully taxable + TDS4.9%₹2,26,700
Endowment Plan5%Tax-free (but terrible returns)5%₹2,44,300

The takeaway:

  • ELSS wins on absolute returns despite being partially taxed — ₹4.1L vs ₹2.98L from PPF over 10 years
  • PPF and SSY win on risk-adjusted, tax-adjusted basis — you keep 100% of returns
  • Tax-saving FD is the worst option — 4.9% post-tax barely beats inflation (6-7%). Your money loses real purchasing power.
  • Endowment plans are a trap — they give 5% returns with 15-20 year lock-in. PPF gives 7.1% with no risk. There's no reason to choose endowment over PPF.

The ₹1.5 Lakh Allocation Strategy — By Profile

You know the options. You know the returns. Now, how should YOU split your ₹1.5L?

Note: These assume your EPF contribution is ₹50,000-₹60,000/year (already within 80C). The allocations below are for the REMAINING ₹90,000-₹1,00,000.

Profile 1: Conservative — Age 50+ or Low Risk Tolerance

InvestmentAmountWhy
PPF₹75,000Tax-free 7.1%, guaranteed returns
NSC₹50,0007.7%, shorter 5-year lock-in
Tax-Saving FD₹25,000Liquidity after 5 years, familiar
Total₹1,50,000Expected blended return: ~6.8%

Add ₹50K in NPS under 80CCD(1B) for extra tax saving.

Profile 2: Balanced — Age 30-50, Some Risk Appetite

InvestmentAmountWhy
ELSS (SIP)₹50,000Wealth creation, shortest lock-in
PPF₹50,000Safe base, tax-free compounding
NPS 80CCD(1B)₹50,000Extra deduction beyond ₹1.5L
Total 80C₹1,00,000 + EPFExpected blended return: ~9.5%

This is the most popular allocation for a reason. You get growth (ELSS), safety (PPF), and extra tax benefit (NPS).

Profile 3: Aggressive — Age 25-35, Long Horizon

InvestmentAmountWhy
ELSS (SIP)₹1,00,000Maximum equity exposure, 12-15% potential
NPS 80CCD(1B)₹50,000Retirement + extra deduction
Total₹1,50,000 + EPFExpected blended return: ~11-13%

At 25-35, you have 30+ years of compounding. A ₹1L ELSS SIP growing at 12% for 30 years becomes ₹29.96 lakh from just ₹30L invested. Time is your biggest asset.

Calculate your ELSS SIP growth →

Profile 4: Parent — Children in School or College

InvestmentAmountWhy
ELSS (SIP)₹50,000Growth for child's higher education
PPF / Sukanya Samriddhi₹50,000Tax-free safe corpus (SSY if you have a daughter)
Tuition Fees₹50,000Already paying — just claim the deduction
Total₹1,50,000Tuition isn't "extra" cost — it's money you're already spending

Don't forget: Tuition fees for up to 2 children are eligible. This includes school fees, college fees, university fees. It does NOT include development fees, donation, transport, or hostel charges.

5 Mistakes That Cost Indians Money Every Year

Mistake 1: Waiting Till March — Cost: ₹5,000-₹10,000

Most people invest in 80C options in the last week of March. Here's what that costs:

  • PPF: If you invest ₹1.5L in April instead of March, you earn 11 extra months of interest = ~₹10,650 over the 15-year term.
  • ELSS: March is usually a market peak (financial year-end rally). April/May often give better entry points.
  • FD: Banks give lower rates in March (they've already met targets). Better rates in April-June.

Fix: Start an ELSS SIP in April. Make your PPF deposit before April 5th. Don't wait.

Mistake 2: Buying Insurance for Tax Saving — Cost: ₹3-5 Lakh Over 20 Years

This is the most expensive mistake Indians make. The math:

  • Endowment plan: ₹50,000/year for 20 years → Maturity: ~₹18-20 lakh (4-5% return)
  • ELSS + Term insurance: ₹40,000/year in ELSS + ₹10,000 for ₹1 crore term plan → ELSS corpus at 12%: ~₹32 lakh PLUS ₹1 crore life cover

The endowment gives you ₹20L with low life cover. ELSS + term gives you ₹32L with ₹1 crore cover. You lose ₹12+ lakh by choosing endowment.

Fix: Buy a pure term plan (₹10K-15K/year for ₹1 crore cover). Invest the rest in ELSS or PPF.

Mistake 3: Ignoring the NPS Extra ₹50,000 — Cost: ₹15,600/Year

Section 80CCD(1B) gives you ₹50,000 deduction OVER AND ABOVE the ₹1.5L limit. Most people don't know this exists.

  • At 30% tax bracket: ₹50,000 x 31.2% (including cess) = ₹15,600 saved
  • Over 25 working years: ₹15,600 x 25 = ₹3,90,000 in extra tax saved
  • Plus the NPS corpus itself grows to ₹35-50 lakh (at 10% returns)

Fix: Open NPS account on enps.nsdl.com. Invest ₹50K/year. Takes 15 minutes.

Mistake 4: Choosing New Regime Without Checking Old Regime — Cost: Varies

The new regime is the default from FY 2024-25. Many Indians just accept the default without running the numbers. As our scenarios above show:

  • At ₹7.5L: New regime wins (correct default)
  • At ₹12L with deductions: Old regime saves ₹39,000+
  • At ₹15L with deductions: Old regime saves ₹80,000+

Fix: Use our tax calculator to compare both regimes with YOUR actual deductions. Takes 2 minutes.

Mistake 5: Not Considering Post-Tax Returns — Cost: 2-3% Return Per Year

People compare "7% FD vs 7.7% NSC" without realising both are fully taxable. After tax at 30%:

  • 7% FD → 4.9% effective
  • 7.7% NSC → 5.4% effective
  • 7.1% PPF → 7.1% effective (tax-free!)

PPF at 7.1% beats both higher-rate options because of EEE status. Always compare post-tax returns, never pre-tax.

Old Regime vs New Regime — The Decision Framework

This question comes up every year. Here's a simple decision tree:

Choose NEW regime if:

  • Your salary is under ₹7.5 lakh (zero tax anyway)
  • You don't pay rent (no HRA exemption)
  • You have no home loan
  • Your only deduction is EPF (no voluntary investments)
  • You can't be bothered with tax planning (the new regime is simpler)

Choose OLD regime if:

  • You pay rent in a metro (HRA exemption is ₹1-2.5L)
  • You have a home loan (₹2L interest deduction under Sec 24b)
  • You invest in PPF/ELSS/NPS (₹1.5L + ₹50K deductions)
  • You have health insurance (₹25K-₹1L under 80D)
  • Your total deductions exceed ₹3.75 lakh (general breakeven point)

The breakeven rule: If your total deductions (including standard deduction of ₹50K in old vs ₹75K in new) exceed approximately ₹3.75 lakh, old regime usually wins. Below that, new regime wins.

Beyond 80C — Other Deductions to Stack

Section 80C is just the start. Here are other deductions that work WITH 80C under the old regime:

SectionDeductionLimitWhat It Covers
80CCD(1B)NPS Extra₹50,000Over and above 80C limit
80DHealth Insurance₹25,000 (self) + ₹50,000 (parents 60+)Premiums paid
80EEducation Loan InterestNo limit (8 years)Interest on higher education loan
80GDonations50% or 100% of donationApproved charities
80TTA/80TTBSavings Interest₹10,000 (₹50,000 for seniors)Interest from savings accounts
24(b)Home Loan Interest₹2,00,000Interest on housing loan
80EEAFirst Home Buyer₹1,50,000Additional home loan interest (if eligible)
80GGRent (no HRA)₹5,000/monthSelf-employed or no HRA component

A salaried person in the 30% bracket can realistically claim ₹5-7 lakh in total deductions (80C + NPS + 80D + HRA + home loan). That's ₹1.5-2.1 lakh saved in tax every year.

Step-by-Step: How to Start Your 80C Investments Today

  1. Check your EPF contribution — Look at your salary slip. Find "Employee PF contribution." Multiply by 12. That's already in your 80C bucket.
  2. Calculate remaining room — ₹1,50,000 minus EPF = your investable 80C amount.
  3. Open a PPF account — Any bank or post office. Online in 10 minutes. Start with ₹500/month.
  4. Start an ELSS SIP — Through Groww, Kuvera, or Coin (Zerodha). Pick a top-rated fund. ₹5,000-₹10,000/month.
  5. Open NPS Tier 1 — Go to enps.nsdl.com. Choose Aggressive lifecycle allocation. Invest ₹50,000 for the 80CCD(1B) extra deduction.
  6. Claim tuition fees — If you have kids in school, keep the fee receipts. Claim at tax filing time.
  7. Don't forget 80D — Buy health insurance if you haven't. ₹25K premium for self + family is deductible.

Calculate your tax savings — Old vs New Regime →

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I claim 80C deduction in the new tax regime?

No. Section 80C deductions are only available under the old tax regime. If you choose the new regime (which is the default from FY 2024-25), you cannot claim any 80C deductions. However, your employer's NPS contribution (80CCD(2), up to 14% of salary) is allowed in both regimes.

What is the maximum 80C deduction for FY 2025-26?

The maximum deduction under Section 80C is ₹1,50,000 per financial year. This limit has remained unchanged since FY 2014-15. This is a combined limit — all your 80C investments together cannot exceed ₹1.5L in deductions.

Is PPF better than ELSS for tax saving?

It depends on your risk appetite and time horizon. PPF gives guaranteed 7.1% tax-free returns with zero risk — ideal for conservative investors. ELSS has historically delivered 10-15% but with market risk and partial taxation (12.5% LTCG above ₹1.25L). For most people, a combination of both is the best approach. Use our PPF vs ELSS calculator to compare for your specific amounts.

Can I invest in 80C options after March 31?

No. The investment must be made within the financial year (April 1 to March 31) to claim deduction for that year. Investments made after March 31 will count for the NEXT financial year. This is why investing early (April-May) is better — you get the full year of returns.

Does home loan EMI count under Section 80C?

Only the principal repayment component of your EMI qualifies under 80C. The interest component is claimed separately under Section 24(b) with a limit of ₹2,00,000 for self-occupied property. Your bank provides a certificate splitting principal and interest — submit this during tax filing.

Is NPS deduction under 80C or separate?

Both. NPS has a unique two-part structure: (1) Your contribution up to 10% of salary is under Section 80CCD(1) WITHIN the ₹1.5L 80C limit. (2) An ADDITIONAL ₹50,000 is deductible under Section 80CCD(1B) — this is OVER AND ABOVE the ₹1.5L limit. So NPS can give you ₹2L total deduction.

Which 80C option gives the highest returns?

ELSS mutual funds have the highest return potential (10-15% CAGR historically), but with market risk. Among risk-free options, Sukanya Samriddhi (8.2%) tops the list, followed by SCSS (8.2% for seniors) and NSC (7.7%). On a post-tax basis, PPF (7.1% completely tax-free) often beats NSC and FD. See our post-tax returns table above.

Can husband and wife both claim Section 80C?

Yes, absolutely. Each individual taxpayer has their own ₹1.5L limit. If both husband and wife are earning and filing taxes, they can each claim up to ₹1.5L — that's ₹3L combined deduction for the family. They can even have separate PPF accounts and ELSS investments. The only condition: you can only claim deduction for investments made from YOUR income, in YOUR name (or jointly where applicable).

What happens if I invest more than ₹1.5 lakh in 80C options?

The extra amount above ₹1.5L does NOT get any tax deduction — but the investment itself is still valid. For example, if you invest ₹2L in PPF (not possible as PPF cap is ₹1.5L, but hypothetically in ELSS), you get ₹1.5L deduction and the remaining ₹50K grows normally with no additional tax benefit. There's no penalty for over-investing — you just don't get extra deduction.

Is EPF contribution automatically counted under 80C?

Yes. Your employee contribution to EPF (12% of basic + DA) is automatically eligible for 80C deduction. Your employer's matching 12% contribution is NOT under 80C (it goes under a separate exemption). Check your Form 16 — your EPF employee contribution will already be listed under 80C.

The Bottom Line

Section 80C is the most powerful tax-saving tool for salaried Indians. But it's not just about saving tax — it's about saving tax while building wealth.

Here's the hierarchy, from best to worst:

  1. ELSS + Term Insurance — Highest returns, lowest lock-in, proper life cover
  2. PPF — Guaranteed tax-free returns, zero risk, excellent for conservative investors
  3. NPS (80CCD 1B) — Extra ₹50K deduction, good returns, retirement corpus
  4. Sukanya Samriddhi — Best risk-free rate (8.2% tax-free), if you have a daughter under 10
  5. NSC — Decent rate, 5-year lock-in, but returns are taxable
  6. Tax-Saving FD — Easy but worst post-tax returns
  7. Endowment/ULIP plans — Avoid unless you have specific reasons

Start in April. Invest via SIP. Don't wait till March. And always compare old vs new regime before filing.

Calculate your exact tax savings now — Income Tax Calculator →

PPF Calculator | ELSS Calculator | NPS Calculator | PPF vs ELSS Comparison | NPS vs PPF Comparison

Sources: Income Tax Act 1961, Finance Act 2025, incometax.gov.in, RBI circulars on small savings rates (Q1 FY2026). Tax calculations verified using official slab rates. Investment returns are historical and not guaranteed.

faq.question: Can I claim 80C deduction in the new tax regime?
faq.answer: No. Section 80C deductions are only available under the old tax regime. If you choose the new regime (default from FY 2024-25), you cannot claim any 80C deductions. However, your employer's NPS contribution (80CCD(2), up to 14% of salary) is allowed in both regimes.

faq.question: What is the maximum 80C deduction for FY 2025-26?
faq.answer: The maximum deduction under Section 80C is ₹1,50,000 per financial year. This limit has remained unchanged since FY 2014-15. This is a combined limit — all your 80C investments together cannot exceed ₹1.5L in deductions.

faq.question: Is PPF better than ELSS for tax saving?
faq.answer: It depends on your risk appetite and time horizon. PPF gives guaranteed 7.1% tax-free returns with zero risk — ideal for conservative investors. ELSS has historically delivered 10-15% but with market risk and partial taxation (12.5% LTCG above ₹1.25L). For most people, a combination of both is the best approach.

faq.question: Can I invest in 80C options after March 31?
faq.answer: No. The investment must be made within the financial year (April 1 to March 31) to claim deduction for that year. Investments made after March 31 will count for the next financial year.

faq.question: Does home loan EMI count under Section 80C?
faq.answer: Only the principal repayment component of your EMI qualifies under 80C. The interest component is claimed separately under Section 24(b) with a limit of ₹2,00,000 for self-occupied property.

faq.question: Is NPS deduction under 80C or separate?
faq.answer: Both. NPS has a unique two-part structure: (1) Your contribution up to 10% of salary is under Section 80CCD(1) WITHIN the ₹1.5L 80C limit. (2) An ADDITIONAL ₹50,000 is deductible under Section 80CCD(1B) — this is OVER AND ABOVE the ₹1.5L limit.

faq.question: Which 80C option gives the highest returns?
faq.answer: ELSS mutual funds have the highest return potential (10-15% CAGR historically), but with market risk. Among risk-free options, Sukanya Samriddhi (8.2%) tops the list, followed by SCSS (8.2% for seniors) and NSC (7.7%). On a post-tax basis, PPF (7.1% completely tax-free) often beats NSC and FD.

faq.question: Can husband and wife both claim Section 80C?
faq.answer: Yes, absolutely. Each individual taxpayer has their own ₹1.5L limit. If both husband and wife are earning and filing taxes, they can each claim up to ₹1.5L — that's ₹3L combined deduction for the family.

faq.question: What happens if I invest more than ₹1.5 lakh in 80C options?
faq.answer: The extra amount above ₹1.5L does NOT get any tax deduction — but the investment itself is still valid. There's no penalty for over-investing — you just don't get extra deduction.

faq.question: Is EPF contribution automatically counted under 80C?
faq.answer: Yes. Your employee contribution to EPF (12% of basic + DA) is automatically eligible for 80C deduction. Your employer's matching 12% contribution is NOT under 80C (it goes under a separate exemption).
